questions.stem: How many ECTS credits is the SB5-MAI Software Maintenance course worth?
questions.explanation: The course is a 5 ECTS course ('0.083 full-time equivalent'). 7.5, 10 and 12.5 are distractors: 12.5 ECTS is actually the size of the advanced OO programming prerequisite, not this course. (Introduction p.4)

questions.stem: What is the stated learning outcome of the SB5-MAI course?

options.text: Greenfield development of new web applications
Maintenance of larger existing software projects
Writing automated test suites from scratch

questions.explanation: The learning outcome is the maintenance of larger existing software projects. The course explicitly assumes you can already write OO software and design systems; its contribution is teaching you to change software you did not write, not greenfield development, cloud design or test authoring. (Introduction p.4)

questions.stem: Roughly how large is the advanced object-oriented programming prerequisite for SB5-MAI?
questions.explanation: Advanced OO programming is listed as ~12.5 ECTS. ~20 ECTS is the size of the software engineering prerequisite, and ~5 ECTS is the size of this course itself, so both are confusable distractors. (Introduction p.4)

questions.stem: Approximately how large is the software engineering prerequisite (process modeling, requirements, analysis, design, architecture) for SB5-MAI?
questions.explanation: The software engineering prerequisite is ~20 ECTS, covering process modeling, requirements, analysis, design and architecture. ~12.5 ECTS is the OO programming prerequisite and ~5 ECTS is this course, making them the natural traps. (Introduction p.4)

questions.stem: In what language(s) can SB5-MAI be taught?
questions.explanation: The teaching language is English or Danish. The slide explicitly offers both, so 'Danish only' and 'English only' are incorrect, and German is never mentioned. (Introduction p.4)

questions.stem: What form does the SB5-MAI examination take?

options.text: An individual written report on campus

questions.explanation: The exam is a written report on campus, based on the individual portfolio. It is individual (not a group defense), written (not oral), and conducted on campus (not take-home), so the other options are wrong. (Introduction p.5)

questions.stem: On which grading scale is the SB5-MAI portfolio assessed?
questions.explanation: The portfolio is assessed on the Danish 7-point grading scale. It is graded (not pass/fail), uses the national 7-point scale rather than a raw percentage, and is not reported on the ECTS A-F letter scale. (Introduction p.5)

questions.stem: What is the concrete portfolio task that the SB5-MAI exam is built around?

options.text: Write a formal specification for JHotDraw
Build a new drawing editor from scratch
Port JHotDraw from Java to Kotlin
Refactor JHotDraw features according to Clean Code

questions.explanation: The portfolio's concrete task is to refactor JHotDraw features according to Clean Code, built from individual mandatory portfolio examples. It is refactoring of an existing codebase, not greenfield construction, a language port, or a formal specification. (Introduction p.6)

questions.stem: From what is the SB5-MAI portfolio built?
questions.explanation: The portfolio is built from individual mandatory portfolio examples. It is individual rather than a group project, example-based rather than exam-based, and academic papers are never part of the assessment. (Introduction p.6)

questions.stem: Which of the following does the course assume you can ALREADY do, so that it can focus on teaching you to change existing software?

options.text: Write OO software and design systems

questions.explanation: The heavy OO and software-engineering prerequisites mean the course assumes you can already write OO software and design systems; its contribution is teaching change. Cluster operations, running CHAOS surveys and compiler authoring are not prerequisites. (Introduction p.4)

questions.stem: Which slogan does the motivation slide use to argue that software pervades modern life?
questions.explanation: The motivation slides say 'Internet in everything' and 'Software is everywhere'. The other slogans are popular tech phrases not used in the deck. (Introduction p.8)

questions.stem: What economic conclusion does the deck draw from the fact that software is everywhere and long-lived?

options.text: Hardware costs now dominate software budgets
Most software effort goes into greenfield development
Most software effort goes into changing, fixing and evolving existing systems

questions.explanation: Because software is pervasive and long-lived, the vast majority of effort is spent changing, fixing and evolving systems already running in the field, which is the economic justification for a maintenance course. The other claims contradict this premise. (Introduction p.8)

questions.stem: How many essential difficulties of software does the Essential Properties slide name?
questions.explanation: The slide names five essential difficulties: complexity, invisibility, changeability, conformity and discontinuity. Three, four and seven are simply wrong counts. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Which set correctly lists all five essential difficulties of software?
questions.explanation: The five essential difficulties are complexity, invisibility, changeability, conformity and discontinuity. The distractors swap in adjacent-sounding terms (coupling/cohesion, scalability, compatibility) that are not on the slide. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Which essential difficulty captures the idea that software has no natural geometric or visual representation?
questions.explanation: Invisibility is the lack of a natural visual/geometric form, which is why we depend on visualization tools. Complexity is about interacting parts, conformity is about external standards, and discontinuity is about disproportionate behavioural changes. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Which essential difficulty refers to software having an enormous number of interacting parts?
questions.explanation: Complexity is the inherently large number of interacting parts (illustrated by a dense web of polygons). Conformity is about external interfaces, changeability about constant pressure to change, and invisibility about lack of visual form. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Which essential difficulty describes software being under constant pressure to change because it is the most malleable part of any system?
questions.explanation: Changeability is the constant pressure to change (illustrated with branching/merging arrows). Discontinuity is about disproportionate behaviour, conformity about external standards, and complexity about interacting parts. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Which essential difficulty explains why software must adapt to arbitrary external interfaces, standards and human institutions it cannot redesign?
questions.explanation: Conformity is the requirement to conform to arbitrary external interfaces and institutions (illustrated as modules M and D linked by a constraint d). Changeability is internal pressure to change, complexity is about parts, and invisibility is about visual form. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Which essential difficulty captures that small input changes can cause disproportionately large, non-continuous changes in behaviour?
questions.explanation: Discontinuity means software does not degrade gracefully like physical systems; small inputs can trigger large jumps (illustrated by a padlock/security icon). The other three difficulties describe parts, pressure to change, and external standards respectively. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Why are the five essential difficulties called 'essential' rather than 'accidental'?

options.text: They only appear in essential (core) modules of a system
They are the difficulties a project must essentially document
They are caused by the essential choice of programming language
They are inherent and cannot be engineered away by tools or languages

questions.explanation: Essential difficulties are inherent and irreducible; no tool or language removes them. They are not tied to 'core' modules, are not a documentation requirement, and are explicitly contrasted with accidental concerns like language choice. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: A student claims 'adopting a better IDE will remove the complexity of our system.' Why is this claim wrong according to the lecture?

options.text: Complexity is an essential difficulty that tooling cannot remove
Complexity is removed only by switching to waterfall
Complexity is an accidental property tied to the IDE
Complexity only matters during the servicing stage

questions.explanation: Complexity is an essential (inherent) difficulty, so no tool, IDE or language removes it; only accidental properties change with tooling. It is not accidental, not stage-specific, and unrelated to choosing waterfall. This is a flagged exam trap. (Introduction p.9-10)

questions.stem: Which of the following is an ACCIDENTAL property of software rather than an essential difficulty?

options.text: The chosen technology/platform of the era

questions.explanation: Accidental properties are incidental, replaceable concerns such as the specific technologies, operating systems and platforms of an era (the slide shows a wall of brand logos). Invisibility, changeability and conformity are essential difficulties. (Introduction p.10)

questions.stem: What is the teaching point of contrasting accidental properties with essential difficulties?

options.text: Accidental properties are the only thing maintenance should target
Essential difficulties disappear once the right platform is chosen
Tool/platform choices change every few years while essential difficulties persist
Accidental properties are harder to solve than essential ones

questions.explanation: Accidental properties (technologies, OSes) change every few years, whereas the essential difficulties persist, so maintenance must target the essential problems and not chase accidental fashion. The other options invert or distort this point. (Introduction p.10)

questions.stem: In which decade did software become a product in its own right, separated from hardware?
questions.explanation: Software was separated from the hardware and software engineering was defined as its own field in the 1950s. The 1940s, 1970s and 1990s are wrong eras for this separation. (Introduction p.11)

questions.stem: From which backgrounds were the earliest programmers recruited during the ad hoc era?
questions.explanation: In the ad hoc era programmers were drawn 'from the ranks of hardware engineers and mathematicians.' The other professions are plausible-sounding but not what the slide states. (Introduction p.14)

questions.stem: What best characterizes the ad hoc paradigm?

options.text: Test-driven design paired with each phase
A strict linear sequence of phases

questions.explanation: The ad hoc paradigm is craft without process: no defined process, drawn from hardware/maths backgrounds. The linear sequence describes waterfall, the repeating loop describes agile, and paired testing describes the V-model. (Introduction p.13)

questions.stem: From which industries is the waterfall model's metaphor borrowed?
questions.explanation: Waterfall is a linear process borrowed from construction and manufacturing, an 'intuitively appealing metaphor.' The other industries are not the source of the waterfall metaphor. (Introduction p.15)

questions.stem: What core assumption of the waterfall model is also its central flaw?

options.text: That every phase must be automated
That the team never understands the code
That requirements are fixed up front

questions.explanation: Waterfall's linear structure assumes requirements are fixed up front, which is its appeal and its flaw because real requirements change. Automation, code understanding and parallel versions are not waterfall's defining assumption. (Introduction p.15)

questions.stem: According to the requirements-volatility anomaly, at roughly what monthly rate do IT requirements change?
questions.explanation: Requirements for IT change at a rate of 2 to 3 % per month (attributed to Capers Jones). The per-software-type breakdown ranges from 1.0% to 3.5% per month around that headline figure, so the other figures are wrong. (Introduction p.16)

questions.stem: In the per-software-type requirements-change table, which software type has the HIGHEST monthly change rate?
questions.explanation: Commercial software changes fastest at 3.5% per month. System and military software are at 2.0%, information systems at 1.5%, and contract/outsource at 1.0%, so commercial is the maximum. (Introduction p.17)

questions.stem: In the per-software-type requirements-change table, which type has the LOWEST monthly change rate?
questions.explanation: Contract/outsource software has the lowest rate at 1.0% per month. Information systems is 1.5%, system and military software 2.0%, and commercial software the highest at 3.5%. (Introduction p.17)

questions.stem: What monthly requirements-change rate do BOTH system software and military software show in the table?
questions.explanation: Both system software and military software change at 2.0% per month. 1.0% is contract/outsource, 1.5% is information systems, and 3.5% is commercial. (Introduction p.17)

questions.stem: Which organization publishes the CHAOS Reports cited as a waterfall anomaly?
questions.explanation: The CHAOS Reports are published by the Standish Group. Capers Jones is associated with the requirements-volatility figure, while the Agile Alliance and Gartner are not the CHAOS publishers. (Introduction p.17)

questions.stem: Since what year have the CHAOS Reports been published annually?
questions.explanation: The CHAOS Reports have been published yearly since 1994. 2001 is the year of the Agile Manifesto (not asked here), 2011 is the start of the FY range for one CHAOS table, and 1984 is too early. (Introduction p.17)

questions.stem: Under the CHAOS definition, a project is counted as 'successful' only if it meets which criteria?

options.text: On time, on budget, with a satisfactory result

questions.explanation: CHAOS scores success by a new definition: on time, on budget, and with a satisfactory result. Documentation, zero defects and the chosen method are not part of the CHAOS success definition. (Introduction p.17)

questions.stem: What repeating cycle does the agile (iterative) paradigm use in place of a single linear pass?
questions.explanation: Agile replaces the single linear pass with a repeating Plan -> Collaborate -> Deliver loop. The other triples are invented or mix in unrelated Git actions. (Introduction p.18)

questions.stem: In the illustrated agile loop, what feeds the iteration?
questions.explanation: A backlog of items feeds an iteration with a daily review, producing a deliverable at each release, with feedback flowing back into the backlog. A frozen spec is waterfall, the V-model is a life-span model, and committed executables are forbidden by the Git lab. (Introduction p.18)

questions.stem: In the CHAOS agile-vs-waterfall table (all sizes), what success rate does agile achieve versus waterfall?
questions.explanation: Across all sizes, agile is 39% successful versus waterfall's 11%. 58% vs 44% is the small-project row, 18% vs 3% is the large-project row, and swapping the figures (11% vs 39%) is wrong. (Introduction p.19)

questions.stem: On large projects, what successful-resolution rates does the CHAOS table report for agile versus waterfall?
questions.explanation: For large projects, agile succeeds 18% of the time versus waterfall's 3% - the most dramatic gap. 39% vs 11% is all sizes, 58% vs 44% is small, and 27% vs 7% is medium. (Introduction p.19)

questions.stem: What is the key qualitative conclusion the summary draws from the paradigm history, despite agile's superior CHAOS numbers?

options.text: Waterfall is always the safest choice for large projects
Agile replaced waterfall and ad hoc entirely
Ad hoc is the only paradigm suited to maintenance
Ad hoc, waterfall and agile co-exist; none erased the others

questions.explanation: The summary insists the three paradigms co-exist; agile did not erase ad hoc or waterfall, each surviving in suitable niches. The other options claim a single paradigm 'won', which the deck explicitly rejects. (Introduction p.27)

questions.stem: What does a software life-span model describe?

options.text: The stages software goes through from conception to death
The four areas of a Git repository
The six reasons to use version control

questions.explanation: A life-span model describes the stages software passes through from conception to death, analogous to other products' life spans. The other options describe Git areas, VCS benefits and essential difficulties respectively. (Introduction p.20-21)

questions.stem: Which set of models does the deck present as software life-span models?
questions.explanation: The life-span models are staged, reengineering, versioned-staged, V-model and prototype. The second list is the agile loop, the third is Git commands, and the fourth is the essential difficulties. (Introduction p.28)

questions.stem: What is the correct order of the five stages in the staged life-span model?

options.text: Initial development -> Servicing -> Evolution -> Phase-out -> Close-down
Initial development -> Evolution -> Phase-out -> Servicing -> Close-down
Initial development -> Evolution -> Servicing -> Phase-out -> Close-down
Evolution -> Initial development -> Servicing -> Close-down -> Phase-out

questions.explanation: The staged model runs Initial development -> Evolution -> Servicing -> Phase-out -> Close-down. The distractors swap evolution/servicing or phase-out/servicing out of order. (Introduction p.21)

questions.stem: What does the initial-development stage of the staged model produce?
questions.explanation: Initial development produces the first version. Retirement is close-down, patches are servicing, and reengineering is a separate counter-force transition. (Introduction p.21)

questions.stem: In the staged model, what distinguishes the Evolution stage from the Servicing stage?

options.text: Evolution makes substantial changes while the team understands the code; servicing applies only small patches
Evolution is for binaries; servicing is for source
Evolution happens after phase-out; servicing happens before initial development
Evolution applies only small patches; servicing makes substantial changes

questions.explanation: Evolution is substantial change (new features, architecture) while the team still understands the code; once evolution stops, servicing applies only smaller patches. The other options invert the distinction or place the stages wrongly. (Introduction p.21)

questions.stem: Which staged-model stage is the JHotDraw portfolio work described as resembling?
questions.explanation: The course's portfolio work on JHotDraw is evolution-style change - real change while the team understands the code - not mere servicing patches, phase-out or close-down. (Introduction p.21)

questions.stem: What force drives the natural drift from Evolution to Servicing in the staged model?
questions.explanation: Code decay drives Evolution -> Servicing: as the codebase degrades, deep evolution becomes too risky. Reengineering/refactoring is the counter-force in the opposite direction, and forking is a Git operation. (Introduction p.22)

questions.stem: What is reengineering in the staged-model overlay, and which transition does it represent?

options.text: The first running version: Initial -> Evolution
The decay that pushes code: Evolution -> Servicing
The counter-force restoring decayed code: Servicing -> Evolution

questions.explanation: Reengineering is the counter-force that restores decayed code, the arrow back from Servicing to Evolution, so real evolution can resume. Evolution -> Servicing is code decay, Initial -> Evolution is the first version, and Phase-out -> Close-down is switch-off. (Introduction p.22)

questions.stem: Why is reengineering/refactoring described as the direct conceptual justification for this course?

options.text: It reverses code decay so software can stay in the evolution stage
It speeds up the close-down of legacy systems
It replaces version control with a central repository
It eliminates the need to understand the existing code

questions.explanation: Reengineering/refactoring fights code decay and keeps software in the evolution stage, which is why a maintenance course exists. It does not accelerate retirement, replace VCS, or remove the need to understand code. (Introduction p.22)

questions.stem: How does the versioned staged model generalize the plain staged model?

options.text: It removes the servicing stage entirely
It runs the staged life cycle in parallel across multiple shipped versions
It merges all versions into one trunk before evolution
It replaces stages with Git branches

questions.explanation: The versioned staged model runs the staged life cycle in parallel across multiple shipped versions, each with its own cycle. It keeps servicing, does not merge versions into one trunk, and is not a Git-branch model. (Introduction p.23)

questions.stem: In the versioned staged model, when is a new evolution branch typically started?

options.text: Only after the old version reaches close-down
Before the old version is closed down
Only during the old version's phase-out

questions.explanation: A new evolution branch is started before the old one is closed down, modelling real products that maintain several supported releases at once. The other options describe strictly sequential versioning, which the model rejects. (Introduction p.23)

questions.stem: What is the defining idea of the V-model?

options.text: Each commit is shared immediately on push
Each stage decays into the next via code decay
Each version ships in parallel with the others
Each constructive activity is paired with a corresponding verification activity

questions.explanation: The V-model pairs each constructive (descending) activity with a corresponding verification (ascending) activity. Parallel versions describe the versioned-staged model, decay belongs to reengineering, and commit/push is Git. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: In the V-model, which test level is paired with the 'requirements' activity?
questions.explanation: Requirements is paired with functional testing. System design is paired with system testing and unit design with unit testing; integration testing is not one of the named V-model pairings. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: In the V-model, which test level is paired with 'system design'?
questions.explanation: System design is paired with system testing. Requirements pairs with functional testing, unit design with unit testing; acceptance testing is not a named V-model level here. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: In the V-model, which test level is paired with 'unit design'?
questions.explanation: Unit design is paired with unit testing. System design pairs with system testing and requirements with functional testing; smoke testing is not a named V-model pairing. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: What sits at the very bottom of the V in the V-model?
questions.explanation: Implementation sits at the bottom of the V, where the descending and ascending arms meet. Maintenance sits at the top, requirements at the top-left, and functional testing at the top-right. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: Where does maintenance appear in the V-model, and what does this signify?

options.text: At the top, as the post-delivery stage above functional testing
On the descending arm, paired with unit design
At the bottom, as the first implementation step
Outside the model, as an optional add-on

questions.explanation: Maintenance appears at the top of the V as the post-delivery stage above functional testing. It is not at the bottom (that is implementation), not on the descending design arm, and not optional. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: What do the dashed horizontal links in the V-model express?

options.text: Each version branches into a new evolution
Each test level validates the corresponding design level
Each commit must be pushed before merging
Each paradigm replaces the previous one

questions.explanation: The dashed horizontal links show that each test level validates the corresponding design level, making testing a first-class planned activity paired with design. The other options describe versioning, Git, and paradigm history. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the prototype in the prototype life-span model?

options.text: To serve as the central version repository for the whole team
To ship the final product directly to end users for release
To surface misunderstandings so corrected requirements drive the real build
To replace the maintenance stage at the end of the life cycle

questions.explanation: The prototype is an early throwaway/learning artifact whose job is to surface misunderstandings so that corrected requirements drive the real build. It is not the shipped product, a maintenance replacement, or a repository. (Introduction p.25)

questions.stem: What is the correct sequence of the prototype model?

options.text: Requirements -> prototype -> corrected requirements -> design -> implementation -> maintenance

questions.explanation: The prototype model runs requirements -> prototype -> corrected requirements -> design -> implementation -> maintenance. The prototype comes before design and produces corrected requirements, so the other orderings are wrong. (Introduction p.25)

questions.stem: How many benefits of version control does the Git Lab list when justifying VCS?
questions.explanation: The Git Lab justifies VCS with six benefits (discipline, archive, history, collaboration, recovery, disk space). Three is the number of essential VCS actions, four is the number of Git areas, and five is the number of essential difficulties. (Git Lab p.3)

questions.stem: Which of the following is one of the six listed benefits of version control?

options.text: Recover from accidental deletions or edits

questions.explanation: Recovering from accidental deletions or edits is one of the six benefits. Compilation, encryption and diagram generation are not among the VCS benefits the slide lists. (Git Lab p.3)

questions.stem: According to the VCS benefits slide, how does version control 'conserve disk space'?

options.text: Each developer keeps only the latest file
Binaries are compressed into the repository
Versions are stored as deltas, not as full copies

questions.explanation: Disk space is conserved because versions are stored as deltas, not full copies. The slide does not say old versions are deleted, that binaries should be stored, or that developers keep only the latest file. (Git Lab p.3)

questions.stem: What are the three essential actions of any version control system?
questions.explanation: The three essential VCS actions are Add (stage), Commit (record), and Update (pull latest). The other triples are real Git commands but are not the three 'essential actions' the slide names. (Git Lab p.4)

questions.stem: In the three essential VCS actions, what does 'Add' do?

options.text: Stage a new or changed file
Record the staged change with a message
Pull the latest state from the repository
Create a complete local copy of the remote

questions.explanation: Add stages a new or changed file. Recording with a message is Commit, pulling latest is Update, and creating a local copy is clone. (Git Lab p.4)

questions.stem: In the three essential VCS actions, what does 'Update' do?

options.text: Record a staged change with a message
Pull the latest state from the repository

questions.explanation: Update pulls the latest state from the repository. Staging is Add, recording is Commit, and pushing is a separate Git action not part of the three essential actions. (Git Lab p.4)

questions.stem: In the generic VCS workflow with two developers editing foo.txt, what is the central repository's role?

options.text: It compiles foo.txt into an executable on each commit
It stores each developer's working copy separately and privately
It is the single synchronisation point each developer commits to and updates from
It prevents either developer from ever seeing the other's changes

questions.explanation: In the generic VCS workflow the central repository is the single synchronisation point: each developer add+commits up to it and updates to receive the other's changes. It does not keep copies private, compile, or block sharing. (Git Lab p.5)

questions.stem: According to the 'Don't do it' slide, which artifact should you NOT put under version control?
questions.explanation: The slide warns against committing executables (e.g. an .EXE) and large binary files (e.g. a 2GB file). Source code, commit messages and plain-text config are exactly what belongs in the repository. (Git Lab p.6)

questions.stem: In the Maven/JHotDraw setup, which directory should specifically NOT be committed, following the 'commit source, not build output' rule?
questions.explanation: The Maven build output lives in target/, which is generated and must never be committed. src/ holds source, .git/ is the repository metadata Git manages, and pom.xml is a source build descriptor that should be committed. (Git Lab p.6)

questions.stem: Into which two types does the Git Lab classify version control systems?
questions.explanation: VCSs are classified as centralized and decentralized/distributed. The other pairings (local/cloud, source/binary, manual/automated) are not the slide's classification. (Git Lab p.7)

questions.stem: What characterizes a centralized VCS?

options.text: Every developer holds a complete local repository containing the project's full history
Commits are never shared with anyone and remain private to each developer forever
A single central repository that every developer talks to directly; the working copy has no full history
There is no remote server at all and work is shared only by copying files manually

questions.explanation: A centralized VCS has a single central repository that all developers talk to directly, and the working copy lacks full history (e.g. CVS, Subversion). Full local history is the decentralized model, and the other options misdescribe both. (Git Lab p.7)

questions.stem: What characterizes a decentralized/distributed VCS?

options.text: There can be only one clone at a time
Only the server keeps the project history
Every developer has a complete local repository with full history

questions.explanation: In a decentralized VCS every clone has a complete local repository with full history, synchronising via a shared remote. The other options describe a centralized model or invent false limitations. (Git Lab p.7)

questions.stem: Which examples does the lecture associate with centralized version control?
questions.explanation: CVS and Subversion are the centralized examples. Git and Mercurial are decentralized, Maven and Gradle are build tools, and GitHub/GitLab are hosting platforms, not VCS types. (Git Lab p.7)

questions.stem: Is Git a centralized or a decentralized VCS?

options.text: Neither; it is a build tool

questions.explanation: Git is explicitly a decentralized VCS in which every clone holds a complete local repository. It is not centralized, not a build tool, and not configuration-dependent in this classification. (Git Lab p.9)

questions.stem: Who created Git?
questions.explanation: Git was created by Linus Torvalds (slide spelling 'Linus Thorvalds'). Ken Thompson is associated with Unix, Guido van Rossum with Python; Junio Hamano maintains Git but did not create it. (Git Lab p.9)

questions.stem: For what original purpose was Git designed?

options.text: To build Java projects with Maven
To replace Subversion in enterprise banking
To manage the Linux kernel / large projects efficiently

questions.explanation: Git was designed to manage large projects efficiently - its original purpose was the Linux kernel. It was not made specifically for banking, is not a Java build tool, and is not a web host. (Git Lab p.9)

questions.stem: From which URL does the lecture say Git can be installed?
questions.explanation: Installation is from http://git-scm.com/downloads via platform package managers. GitHub is a host, Maven is a build tool, and linux.org is not the Git download site. (Git Lab p.10)

questions.stem: What is the correct ordering of the four Git areas from your disk outward to the server?

options.text: Workspace -> local repository (HEAD) -> index/stage -> remote repository
Workspace -> index/stage -> local repository (HEAD) -> remote repository
Remote repository -> local repository -> workspace -> index/stage
Index/stage -> workspace -> remote repository -> local repository

questions.explanation: The four areas, from disk outward, are workspace -> index/stage -> local repository (HEAD) -> remote repository. The other orderings shuffle these areas out of sequence. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: In the Git four-area model, what is the 'index'?

options.text: The remote repository acting as the shared server copy
The local repository holding your committed history
The staging area of changes selected for the next commit
The working directory holding your actual files on disk

questions.explanation: The index/stage is the staging area of changes selected for the next commit. Files on disk are the workspace, committed history is the local repository (HEAD), and the server copy is the remote. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: In the Git four-area model, what does 'HEAD' refer to?

options.text: The staging area for the next commit

questions.explanation: HEAD denotes the local repository - your committed history stored locally (decentralized). The staging area is the index, the server copy is the remote, and disk files are the workspace. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which Git command copies a remote repository into a full local copy of everything?
questions.explanation: clone moves the remote repository to the workspace, a full local copy of everything. fetch downloads without merging, pull is fetch+merge, and add stages files. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: In the Git command map, what does 'add' do?

options.text: Moves staged changes into the local repository
Moves changes from the workspace into the index/stage
Moves local commits to the remote

questions.explanation: add moves changes from the workspace into the index/stage (with -u staging already-tracked modified files). Staged -> local repo is commit, local -> remote is push, and remote -> local is fetch. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: In the Git command map, what does 'commit' do?

options.text: Moves remote commits into the workspace
Moves local commits to the remote repository
Moves staged changes from the index into the local repository (HEAD)
Moves workspace changes into the index

questions.explanation: commit moves staged changes from the index/stage into the local repository (HEAD). Local -> remote is push, remote -> workspace is pull, and workspace -> index is add. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: In the Git command map, what does 'push' do?

options.text: Moves staged changes into the local repository
Moves commits from the local repository to the remote repository

questions.explanation: push moves commits from the local repository to the remote repository. Index -> local is commit, download-without-merge is fetch, and staging is add. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which Git command is equivalent to 'fetch' followed by 'merge' in one step?
questions.explanation: pull = fetch + merge, bringing remote changes into the workspace in one step. clone creates a fresh local copy, push uploads, and commit records locally. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: What does 'git diff HEAD' compare?

options.text: The workspace against the last commit (HEAD)
The index against the remote repository

questions.explanation: diff HEAD compares the workspace against the last commit (HEAD). Plain diff compares the workspace against the index/stage; the other options misstate what diff compares. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: What does plain 'git diff' (without HEAD) compare?

options.text: The staged changes against the remote
The local repository against the remote
The workspace against the last commit

questions.explanation: Plain diff compares the workspace against the index/stage. Comparing against the last commit is diff HEAD; the local-vs-remote and staged-vs-remote comparisons are not what diff does here. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Why is understanding that 'commit' is local and 'push' is what shares it called the single most exam-relevant Git concept?

options.text: It is what determines which branch the HEAD pointer currently points at in the repository
It is the setting that controls whether the Maven build skips tests during a smoke build
It is the only mechanism by which Git is able to conserve disk space across versions
It is the defining feature of a decentralized VCS: teammates see nothing until you push

questions.explanation: commit records only to your local HEAD; teammates see nothing until you push - the defining feature of a decentralized VCS and a classic exam trap. It is unrelated to disk space, HEAD's branch, or test-skipping. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: A developer runs 'git fetch' but their working files are unchanged. What did they forget, and why?

options.text: They forgot to merge; fetch downloads to the local repo but does not update the workspace
They forgot to commit; fetch needs a prior commit to take effect
They forgot to clone; fetch requires re-cloning each time
They forgot to push; fetch only works after pushing

questions.explanation: fetch only downloads commits into the local repository; the workspace stays unchanged until you merge (pull = fetch + merge). It does not require a prior commit, a push, or re-cloning. This is a flagged gotcha. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: In the example local-repo workflow, which command shows which files you changed?
questions.explanation: git status shows which files you changed. git diff shows the actual changes, git show displays the last commit, and git push uploads to the remote. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: In the example local-repo workflow, which command shows the actual content of your changes?
questions.explanation: git diff [files] shows the changes themselves. git status lists which files changed, git show displays the last commit, and git clone copies a remote repository. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: Which command does the local-repo slide use to view the most recent commit?
questions.explanation: git show is used to view the most recent (last) commit. git status lists changed files, git diff shows uncommitted changes, and git fetch downloads from the remote. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: Per the documented best practice, what should you always run before committing?
questions.explanation: Always git status then git diff before committing so you know exactly what you are recording. Skipping this inspection loop is a flagged pitfall; the other sequences are unrelated to pre-commit inspection. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: What does 'git clone <repository>' create and link to?

options.text: An empty repository awaiting git init
A complete local copy linked to the remote named origin
A shallow copy with no history, linked to upstream
A central repository for the whole team

questions.explanation: clone creates a complete local copy of the repository and links it to the remote, named origin. It is not a history-less shallow copy, not a central team repo, and not an empty init. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: By default, what name is given to the remote that 'git clone' links to?
questions.explanation: The default remote name created by clone is origin. upstream is a convention for a second remote, main is a branch name, and head/HEAD is the current commit pointer. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: Which URL schemes can a clone <repository> argument use, per the remote-workflow slide?
questions.explanation: The <repository> can be a URL using file, http, ssh and similar schemes. The mail/transport-layer protocols listed in the other options are not used to clone, and clone is not restricted to a single scheme. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: On the remote-workflow slide, which command receives changes from the server?
questions.explanation: git pull receives changes from the server. git push sends changes, git add stages, and git status lists changed files. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: On the remote-workflow slide, which command sends your changes to the server?
questions.explanation: git push sends your changes to the server. git pull and git fetch receive changes, and git commit only records locally. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: What is GitHub introduced as in the Git Lab?

options.text: A hosting platform for remote repositories
A decentralized VCS that replaces Git

questions.explanation: GitHub is introduced as the hosting platform for remote repositories. It is not a VCS replacing Git, not a build system (that is Maven), and not a drawing framework (that is JHotDraw). (Git Lab p.16)

questions.stem: Which project does the Git Lab's 'Projects' slide name as the course case study?
questions.explanation: The Projects slide names JHotDraw as the course project and recurring case study. JFreeChart, Maven and Subversion appear nowhere as the case study. (Git Lab p.18)

questions.stem: What kind of software is JHotDraw?
questions.explanation: JHotDraw is a Java drawing-editor framework, also a teaching vehicle for design patterns. It is not a VCS, a CI server, or a database. (Git Lab p.18)

questions.stem: In the 'LAB TODOs', after cloning JHotDraw and learning Git, what does the lab tell students to file on GitHub?

options.text: A change request as an Issue

questions.explanation: LAB TODOs tell students to file a change request as an Issue on GitHub - the first taste of course Initiation, a change request entering the system. It is not a pull request, a release tag, or a wiki page. (Git Lab p.19)

questions.stem: Filing a GitHub Issue for a JHotDraw change is described as the course's first instance of which canonical change-process phase?
questions.explanation: Filing a change request as a GitHub Issue is the course's first instance of Initiation: a change request enters the backlog before any code is touched. Concept Location, Impact Analysis and Actualization are later phases. (Git Lab p.19)

questions.stem: In the IntroLab, how does each team obtain its own copy of the JHotDraw repository to work on?

options.text: Each team mirrors it to a centralized SVN server
Each member clones directly from the original without forking
The instructor emails a zip of the source
Each team forks the project repository on GitHub

questions.explanation: Each team forks the JHotDraw repository on GitHub, then members follow GitHub flow on their own branches. There is no direct-clone-only, no emailed zip, and no SVN mirror in the lab. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which collaboration model do JHotDraw team members follow after forking?

options.text: GitHub flow, each on their own feature branch
A single shared branch everyone commits to

questions.explanation: Members follow GitHub flow, each working on their own feature branch so collaboration never collides. A single shared branch, branchless trunk development, and lock-based check-in are not the prescribed model. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which Maven and JDK versions does the IntroLab specify for building JHotDraw?
questions.explanation: The IntroLab specifies Maven 3.8.x on JDK 11. The other version combinations (Maven 4 / JDK 17, Maven 3.6 / JDK 8, Gradle) are not what the lab prescribes. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: From the JHotDraw project root, which command does the IntroLab use for the first smoke build?
questions.explanation: From the project root the lab runs mvn clean install -DskipTests, compiling and installing all modules while skipping tests for the first smoke build. The other Maven invocations are not the prescribed command. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: Why does the IntroLab's first build use the '-DskipTests' flag?

options.text: To skip downloading Maven dependencies and build entirely from a local cache
To permanently disable testing for the whole course so tests never run again
To run only the project's tests while skipping compilation of the source modules
To get a fast smoke build that confirms compilation without running the test suite

questions.explanation: -DskipTests gives a fast smoke build that confirms the modules compile and install without running tests. It does not permanently disable tests, skip dependency download, or invert into tests-only. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: From which module folder does the IntroLab run the JHotDraw GUI?
questions.explanation: The GUI is run from the jhotdraw-samples-misc folder. jhotdraw-core, jhotdraw-app and jhotdraw-svg-main are not the folder the lab names for launching the sample editor. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which exact main class does the IntroLab launch to open the JHotDraw SVG sample GUI?
questions.explanation: The GUI is launched with mvn exec:java targeting org.jhotdraw.samples.svg.Main; forgetting the exact class means the GUI will not appear (a flagged pitfall). The other class names are plausible but wrong. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: What appears after running the JHotDraw exec:java command successfully?

options.text: The JHotDraw SVG sample editor GUI

questions.explanation: On success the JHotDraw GUI - the SVG sample editor - appears, confirming a working environment. It is not a REPL, a web server, or a PDF report. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: In the day-one worked example, what is the correct order of the local inspect-and-record loop after editing a file?

options.text: git add -u -> git status -> git commit -> git diff
git commit -> git add -u -> git diff -> git status
git diff -> git commit -> git status -> git add -u
git status -> git diff -> git add -u -> git commit

questions.explanation: The loop is git status (which files changed) -> git diff (what changed) -> git add -u (stage tracked changes) -> git commit (record to HEAD). The other orderings commit or stage before inspecting. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: After committing locally on a feature branch, how do teammates integrate each other's JHotDraw work?

options.text: One developer pushes the branch; teammates pull (fetch + merge)
They email patches and apply them by hand
The instructor manually copies files between machines
Each developer re-clones the repository from scratch

questions.explanation: You push your branch to origin and teammates pull (= fetch + merge) to integrate. Re-cloning, manual copying and emailing patches are not the prescribed sharing mechanism. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: A teammate says 'I committed my fix an hour ago but I still can't see it.' What is the most likely explanation?

options.text: The fix was pushed but never committed
The fix was committed locally but never pushed to the remote
The fix was merged but never fetched
The repository is centralized so commits are invisible

questions.explanation: In a decentralized VCS, commit records only to the local HEAD; teammates see nothing until a push, so the unpushed commit is invisible. 'Pushed but not committed' is impossible, the merge/fetch option is garbled, and Git is decentralized regardless. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which pairing of concept to its category is correct?

options.text: Discontinuity = accidental property; changeability = accidental property
Invisibility = accidental property; chosen OS = essential difficulty
Invisibility = essential difficulty; chosen OS = accidental property
Complexity = accidental property; conformity = accidental property

questions.explanation: Invisibility is an essential difficulty, while the chosen operating system is an accidental property. The other options miscategorize essential difficulties as accidental or vice versa. (Introduction p.9-10)

questions.stem: Which three paradigms does the lecture trace through software-engineering history?
questions.explanation: The three paradigms are ad hoc, waterfall and agile/iterative. The second list is life-span models, the third is VCS types, and the fourth is the agile loop's internal phases. (Introduction p.12-19)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly contrasts the staged model's Servicing stage with the V-model's maintenance node?

options.text: Servicing is a reduced stage of patch-only change; V-model maintenance is the post-delivery stage at the top of the V
Servicing sits at the bottom of the V, while maintenance is what produces the very first version
They are simply two identical names for the same initial-development stage in either model
Both denote the very first stage of their respective models, before any implementation begins

questions.explanation: Servicing in the staged model is the reduced patch-only stage after evolution stops, while the V-model places maintenance at the top as the post-delivery stage. They are neither first stages, nor identical, nor at the bottom of the V. (Introduction p.21-24)

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly distinguishes what each Git action shares with teammates?

options.text: both commit and push share changes with the remote
commit keeps changes local; push shares them with the remote
commit shares changes with the remote; push keeps them local
both commit and push keep changes local

questions.explanation: commit keeps changes in your local repository, and only push shares them with the remote. The other options either invert the two or wrongly claim both behave the same. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which life-span model inserts an early throwaway artifact to fix requirements before design?
questions.explanation: The prototype model inserts an early throwaway/learning artifact to correct requirements before design. The staged model sequences stages, the V-model pairs design with testing, and the versioned model runs versions in parallel. (Introduction p.25)

questions.stem: Which statement about the CHAOS Reports is correct?

options.text: They are published by Capers Jones every two years
They were first published in 2011 and track ~10,000 projects
They define success purely as zero post-release defects
They have been published yearly since 1994 and track ~50,000 projects

questions.explanation: The CHAOS Reports have run yearly since 1994 tracking ~50,000 projects, with success defined as on time, on budget, satisfactory result. They are not from 2011, not by Capers Jones, and success is not 'zero defects'. (Introduction p.17)

questions.stem: Although Lecture 1 does not walk through the canonical change process, which phase ordering does it foreshadow for portfolio changes?

options.text: Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Verification
Actualization -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Verification
Prefactoring -> Postfactoring -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis
Impact Analysis -> Concept Location -> Actualization -> Verification -> Prefactoring

questions.explanation: The guide notes every portfolio change should pass through Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Verification, named in later lectures. The other orderings scramble these phases. (Introduction p.6)

questions.stem: Which area in the Git four-area model holds your actual files on disk?
questions.explanation: The workspace (working dir) holds your actual files on disk. The index is the staging area, HEAD is committed local history, and the remote is the server copy. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which area in the Git four-area model is the shared server copy, e.g. on GitHub?
questions.explanation: The remote repository (origin) is the shared server copy, e.g. on GitHub. The workspace is disk files, the index is staging, and HEAD is local committed history. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the three software-engineering paradigms in the lecture?
questions.explanation: Prototype is a life-span model, not one of the three paradigms (ad hoc, waterfall, agile/iterative). The other three are exactly the paradigms the lecture traces. (Introduction p.12-19)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the software life-span models in the lecture?
questions.explanation: Agile is a paradigm, not a life-span model. Staged, V-model and prototype (plus reengineering and versioned-staged) are the life-span models. (Introduction p.28)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the five essential difficulties?
questions.explanation: Scalability is not among the five essential difficulties (complexity, invisibility, changeability, conformity, discontinuity). The other three are genuine members of the set. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Which best explains why the lecture stresses that the three paradigms 'co-exist'?

options.text: It means waterfall is now banned for all project sizes
Co-existence means they must always be used together on every project
Each paradigm still fits some contexts, so none was simply replaced
All three were merged into a single modern process

questions.explanation: Co-existence means each paradigm survives in suitable niches and none was simply replaced; it does not mean they merged, must all be used together, or that waterfall is banned. (Introduction p.27)

questions.stem: What is the index/stage used for in Git?

options.text: Storing the full project history locally
Holding the server's copy of the repository
Keeping the working files you edit on disk
Selecting which changes go into the next commit

questions.explanation: The index/stage selects which changes go into the next commit. Full local history is HEAD/local repo, the server copy is the remote, and edited files on disk are the workspace. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly maps each Git command to the four-area transition it performs?

options.text: clone: remote->workspace; commit: index->local; push: local->remote; pull: remote->workspace
clone: index->workspace; commit: remote->local; push: workspace->index; pull: local->remote
clone: workspace->remote; commit: local->index; push: remote->local; pull: workspace->remote
clone: local->remote; commit: workspace->remote; push: index->workspace; pull: remote->index

questions.explanation: clone goes remote->workspace, commit index->local, push local->remote, and pull remote->workspace. The other options scramble the source/destination areas of each command. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: What does the deck say software was 'separated from' when it became a product in its own right?
questions.explanation: Software was 'separated from the hardware' in the 1950s, becoming a product and field of its own. It was not framed as separated from the OS, network or UI. (Introduction p.11)

questions.stem: What does 'git show' display in the local-repo workflow?
questions.explanation: git show displays the last commit. Listing changed files is git status, diffing against the index is git diff, and remote branches are shown by other commands. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: Which sequence reflects the IntroLab's intended order for getting JHotDraw running?

options.text: mvn clean install -> fork -> clone -> run svg Main
Clone -> fork -> run svg Main -> mvn clean install
Run svg Main -> clone -> fork -> mvn clean install
Fork on GitHub -> clone -> mvn clean install -DskipTests -> run svg Main

questions.explanation: The intended order is fork on GitHub, clone the fork, build with mvn clean install -DskipTests, then run org.jhotdraw.samples.svg.Main. The other orderings put build or run before the repository even exists locally. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: On the Essential Properties slide, which difficulty is illustrated with branching/merging arrows (suggesting version-control branches)?
questions.explanation: Changeability is illustrated with branching/merging arrows, evoking version-control branches under constant pressure to change. Invisibility, conformity and discontinuity use other illustrations. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: On the Essential Properties slide, conformity is illustrated as which of the following?

options.text: Modules M and D connected by a constraint d

questions.explanation: Conformity is illustrated as modules M and D connected by a constraint d (an external interface to conform to). The polygon web is complexity, the padlock is discontinuity, and arrows are changeability. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: On the Essential Properties slide, complexity is illustrated by which image?

options.text: A dense web of polygons ('all shapes')

questions.explanation: Complexity is illustrated by a dense web of polygons ('all shapes'). The padlock is discontinuity, arrows are changeability, and the wall of brand logos illustrates accidental properties, not complexity. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: The wall of brand logos (GWT, Joomla, WordPress, AJAX, .NET, etc.) illustrates which concept?
questions.explanation: The wall of brand logos illustrates accidental properties - the replaceable technologies/platforms of an era. It is not the essential difficulties, the agile loop, or the Git areas. (Introduction p.10)

questions.stem: Which threads does the lecture say recur all semester, connected from day one?

options.text: A legacy codebase (JHotDraw), a disciplined change workflow, and version control
A compiler project, a waterfall plan, and manual file copying
A database schema, a UML diagram, and a release tag
A new greenfield app, a fixed specification, and centralized SVN

questions.explanation: From day one the course connects a real legacy codebase (JHotDraw), a disciplined change workflow (the canonical change process), and version control as the substrate for traceable change. The other options describe unrelated artifacts. (Introduction p.6)

questions.stem: The summary slide restates that software engineers face essential difficulties of which five kinds?
questions.explanation: The summary restates complexity, invisibility, changeability, conformity and discontinuity. The other lists substitute project-management or adjacent terms not on the slide. (Introduction p.27)

questions.stem: Why does the guide treat the 'file a GitHub Issue' step as conceptually important beyond Git mechanics?

options.text: It replaces Impact Analysis entirely so no dependency assessment is needed before changing
It is the final Verification step of the change process, recorded after the code is complete
It is the course's first Initiation step: a change request entering the system before code changes
It is identical to pushing a feature branch and shares the same commit with the whole team

questions.explanation: Filing a GitHub Issue is the first Initiation step - a change request enters the system before any code is touched. It is not Verification, not the same as pushing a branch, and does not replace Impact Analysis. (Git Lab p.19)

questions.stem: What is JHotDraw used as throughout the entire SB5-MAI course?

options.text: The recurring case study and portfolio target

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is the recurring case study and the target of all portfolio work. It is not the VCS server (GitHub), the grading tool, or the build system (Maven). (Git Lab p.18)

questions.stem: Which describes the relationship between the agile loop and the canonical change process, per the guide?

options.text: The iterative change loop is the spiritual ancestor of the canonical change process
The canonical change process predates and contradicts agile
The agile loop replaces the canonical change process
They are unrelated and never connected in the course

questions.explanation: The guide links the iterative (agile) change loop as the spiritual ancestor of the canonical change process. It does not replace it, predate-and-contradict it, or stand unrelated. (Introduction p.19)

questions.stem: Which command records staged changes into your local repository?
questions.explanation: git commit records staged changes into your local repository (HEAD). push uploads to the remote, clone copies a remote, and fetch downloads commits. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which command uploads your local commits to the remote repository?
questions.explanation: git push uploads your local commits to the remote. commit only records locally, add stages, and diff compares changes. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Two developers commit to their own local repositories but never push. What is true about the central remote?

options.text: The remote merges their work for them
The remote automatically receives both sets of commits
The remote has neither developer's new commits until someone pushes

questions.explanation: Because commit is local in a decentralized VCS, the remote has neither set of commits until someone pushes; it does not auto-receive, auto-merge, or reject clones. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Given the requirements-volatility data, why does a fixed up-front specification become problematic on a multi-month project?

options.text: At 2-3% change per month, the spec is obsolete before delivery
Maven cannot build against a specification

questions.explanation: At 2-3% requirements change per month, a multi-month project's fixed up-front spec is obsolete before delivery - waterfall's core problem. The other options are irrelevant to the volatility argument. (Introduction p.16)

questions.stem: Which combination of fixed technical environment does day-one setup establish for the whole course?
questions.explanation: Day-one setup establishes JHotDraw + Maven + Git/GitHub as the fixed technical environment for the whole course. The other combinations swap in the wrong build tool, VCS, or project. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which statement best captures how Lecture 1 relates to the canonical change process taught later?

options.text: It explicitly names Brooks as the author of the change process
It replaces the change process with the agile loop
It sets up the course and implies the process via the portfolio brief, but does not itself walk through the process
It fully walks through Concept Location through Verification step by step

questions.explanation: Lecture 1 sets up the course and implies the change process via the portfolio brief ('refactor JHotDraw according to Clean Code') but does not itself walk through the canonical phases - those come later. It does not detail the phases, name Brooks for the process, or replace it. (Introduction p.6)

questions.stem: An exam answer states 'switching from PHP to Java will solve our conformity problem.' Why is this wrong?

options.text: Conformity is an accidental property fixed by changing language
Conformity only applies to centralized VCS
Java has no conformity constraints at all
Conformity is an essential difficulty; the language is an accidental property and cannot remove it

questions.explanation: Conformity is an essential difficulty - the need to conform to external interfaces/institutions - while the language is accidental, so a language switch cannot remove it. The other options misclassify conformity or invent false claims. (Introduction p.9-10)

questions.stem: Which is the correct reason waterfall struggles, drawn from BOTH anomalies the deck presents?

options.text: Requirements change continuously (~2-3%/month) and CHAOS data show high challenged/failed rates
Disk space runs out and developers cannot clone
Agile is legally mandated for all projects
Construction metaphors are illegal in software

questions.explanation: Waterfall struggles because requirements change continuously (~2-3%/month) and the CHAOS data show large fractions of projects challenged or failed - the two empirical anomalies. The other options are irrelevant. (Introduction p.16-17)
